Hell and night Must bring this monstrous birth to the world's light.

lines: Sir, would she give you so much of her lips
As of her tongue she oft bestows on me,
You would have enough.
fullText: Sir, would she give you so much of her lips As of her tongue she oft bestows on me, You would have enough.

lines: In faith, too much.
I find it still when I have list to sleep.
Marry, before your Ladyship, I grant,
She puts her tongue a little in her heart
And chides with thinking.
fullText: In faith, too much. I find it still when I have list to sleep. Marry, before your Ladyship, I grant, She puts her tongue a little in her heart And chides with thinking.

lines: Come on, come on! You are pictures out of door,
bells in your parlors, wildcats in your kitchens,
saints in your injuries, devils being offended, players
in your huswifery, and huswives in your beds.
fullText: Come on, come on! You are pictures out of door, bells in your parlors, wildcats in your kitchens, saints in your injuries, devils being offended, players in your huswifery, and huswives in your beds.

lines: Nay, it is true, or else I am a Turk.
You rise to play, and go to bed to work.
fullText: Nay, it is true, or else I am a Turk. You rise to play, and go to bed to work.

lines: No, let me not.
fullText: No, let me not.

lines: O, gentle lady, do not put me to 't,
For I am nothing if not critical.
fullText: O, gentle lady, do not put me to 't, For I am nothing if not critical.

lines: Ay, madam.
fullText: Ay, madam.

lines: I am about it, but indeed my invention comes
from my pate as birdlime does from frieze: it
plucks out brains and all. But my muse labors, and
thus she is delivered:
If she be fair and wise, fairness and wit,
The one's for use, the other useth it.
fullText: I am about it, but indeed my invention comes from my pate as birdlime does from frieze: it plucks out brains and all. But my muse labors, and thus she is delivered: If she be fair and wise, fairness and wit, The one's for use, the other useth it.

lines: If she be black, and thereto have a wit,
She'll find a white that shall her blackness hit.
fullText: If she be black, and thereto have a wit, She'll find a white that shall her blackness hit.

lines: She never yet was foolish that was fair,
For even her folly helped her to an heir.
fullText: She never yet was foolish that was fair, For even her folly helped her to an heir.

lines: There's none so foul and foolish thereunto,
But does foul pranks which fair and wise ones do.
fullText: There's none so foul and foolish thereunto, But does foul pranks which fair and wise ones do.

lines: She that was ever fair and never proud,
Had tongue at will and yet was never loud,
Never lacked gold and yet went never gay,
Fled from her wish, and yet said "Now I may,"
She that being angered, her revenge being nigh,
Bade her wrong stay and her displeasure fly,
She that in wisdom never was so frail
To change the cod's head for the salmon's tail,
She that could think and ne'er disclose her mind,
See suitors following and not look behind,
She was a wight, if ever such wight were--
fullText: She that was ever fair and never proud, Had tongue at will and yet was never loud, Never lacked gold and yet went never gay, Fled from her wish, and yet said "Now I may," She that being angered, her revenge being nigh, Bade her wrong stay and her displeasure fly, She that in wisdom never was so frail To change the cod's head for the salmon's tail, She that could think and ne'er disclose her mind, See suitors following and not look behind, She was a wight, if ever such wight were--

lines: To suckle fools and chronicle small beer.
fullText: To suckle fools and chronicle small beer.

lines: He takes her by the palm. Ay, well said,
whisper. With as little a web as this will I ensnare as
great a fly as Cassio. Ay, smile upon her, do. I will
gyve thee in thine own courtship. You say true, 'tis
so indeed. If such tricks as these strip you out of
your lieutenantry, it had been better you had not
kissed your three fingers so oft, which now again
you are most apt to play the sir in. Very good; well
kissed; an excellent courtesy! 'Tis so, indeed. Yet
again your fingers to your lips? Would they were
clyster pipes for your sake!
The Moor. I know his trumpet.
fullText: He takes her by the palm. Ay, well said, whisper. With as little a web as this will I ensnare as great a fly as Cassio. Ay, smile upon her, do. I will gyve thee in thine own courtship. You say true, 'tis so indeed. If such tricks as these strip you out of your lieutenantry, it had been better you had not kissed your three fingers so oft, which now again you are most apt to play the sir in. Very good; well kissed; an excellent courtesy! 'Tis so, indeed. Yet again your fingers to your lips? Would they were clyster pipes for your sake! The Moor. I know his trumpet.

lines: O, you are well tuned now,
But I'll set down the pegs that make this music,
As honest as I am.
fullText: O, you are well tuned now, But I'll set down the pegs that make this music, As honest as I am.

lines: Do thou meet me presently
at the harbor. Come hither. If
thou be'st valiant--as they say base men being in
love have then a nobility in their natures more than
is native to them--list me. The Lieutenant tonight
watches on the court of guard. First, I must tell thee
this: Desdemona is directly in love with him.
fullText: Do thou meet me presently at the harbor. Come hither. If thou be'st valiant--as they say base men being in love have then a nobility in their natures more than is native to them--list me. The Lieutenant tonight watches on the court of guard. First, I must tell thee this: Desdemona is directly in love with him.

lines: Lay thy finger thus, and let thy soul be instructed.
Mark me with what violence she first loved the
Moor but for bragging and telling her fantastical
lies. And will she love him still for prating? Let not
thy discreet heart think it. Her eye must be fed. And
what delight shall she have to look on the devil?
When the blood is made dull with the act of sport,
there should be, again to inflame it and to give
satiety a fresh appetite, loveliness in favor, sympathy
in years, manners, and beauties, all which the Moor
is defective in. Now, for want of these required
conveniences, her delicate tenderness will find itself
abused, begin to heave the gorge, disrelish and
abhor the Moor. Very nature will instruct her in it
and compel her to some second choice. Now, sir,
this granted--as it is a most pregnant and unforced
position--who stands so eminent in the degree of
this fortune as Cassio does? A knave very voluble, no
further conscionable than in putting on the mere
form of civil and humane seeming for the better
compassing of his salt and most hidden loose
affection. Why, none, why, none! A slipper and
subtle knave, a finder-out of occasions, that has an
eye can stamp and counterfeit advantages, though
true advantage never present itself; a devilish knave!
Besides, the knave is handsome, young, and hath all
those requisites in him that folly and green minds
look after. A pestilent complete knave, and the
woman hath found him already.
fullText: Lay thy finger thus, and let thy soul be instructed. Mark me with what violence she first loved the Moor but for bragging and telling her fantastical lies. And will she love him still for prating? Let not thy discreet heart think it. Her eye must be fed. And what delight shall she have to look on the devil? When the blood is made dull with the act of sport, there should be, again to inflame it and to give satiety a fresh appetite, loveliness in favor, sympathy in years, manners, and beauties, all which the Moor is defective in. Now, for want of these required conveniences, her delicate tenderness will find itself abused, begin to heave the gorge, disrelish and abhor the Moor. Very nature will instruct her in it and compel her to some second choice. Now, sir, this granted--as it is a most pregnant and unforced position--who stands so eminent in the degree of this fortune as Cassio does? A knave very voluble, no further conscionable than in putting on the mere form of civil and humane seeming for the better compassing of his salt and most hidden loose affection. Why, none, why, none! A slipper and subtle knave, a finder-out of occasions, that has an eye can stamp and counterfeit advantages, though true advantage never present itself; a devilish knave! Besides, the knave is handsome, young, and hath all those requisites in him that folly and green minds look after. A pestilent complete knave, and the woman hath found him already.

lines: Blessed fig's end! The wine she drinks is made of
grapes. If she had been blessed, she would never
have loved the Moor. Blessed pudding! Didst thou
not see her paddle with the palm of his hand? Didst
not mark that?
fullText: Blessed fig's end! The wine she drinks is made of grapes. If she had been blessed, she would never have loved the Moor. Blessed pudding! Didst thou not see her paddle with the palm of his hand? Didst not mark that?

lines: Lechery, by this hand! An index and obscure
prologue to the history of lust and foul thoughts.
They met so near with their lips that their breaths
embraced together. Villainous thoughts, Roderigo!
When these mutualities so marshal the way, hard
at hand comes the master and main exercise, th'
incorporate conclusion. Pish! But, sir, be you ruled
by me. I have brought you from Venice. Watch you
tonight. For the command, I'll lay 't upon you.
Cassio knows you not. I'll not be far from you. Do
you find some occasion to anger Cassio, either by
speaking too loud, or tainting his discipline, or from
what other course you please, which the time shall
more favorably minister.
fullText: Lechery, by this hand! An index and obscure prologue to the history of lust and foul thoughts. They met so near with their lips that their breaths embraced together. Villainous thoughts, Roderigo! When these mutualities so marshal the way, hard at hand comes the master and main exercise, th' incorporate conclusion. Pish! But, sir, be you ruled by me. I have brought you from Venice. Watch you tonight. For the command, I'll lay 't upon you. Cassio knows you not. I'll not be far from you. Do you find some occasion to anger Cassio, either by speaking too loud, or tainting his discipline, or from what other course you please, which the time shall more favorably minister.

lines: Sir, he's rash and very sudden in choler, and
haply may strike at you. Provoke him that he may,
for even out of that will I cause these of Cyprus to
mutiny, whose qualification shall come into no
true taste again but by the displanting of Cassio. So
shall you have a shorter journey to your desires by
the means I shall then have to prefer them, and the
impediment most profitably removed, without the
which there were no expectation of our prosperity.
fullText: Sir, he's rash and very sudden in choler, and haply may strike at you. Provoke him that he may, for even out of that will I cause these of Cyprus to mutiny, whose qualification shall come into no true taste again but by the displanting of Cassio. So shall you have a shorter journey to your desires by the means I shall then have to prefer them, and the impediment most profitably removed, without the which there were no expectation of our prosperity.

lines: I warrant thee. Meet me by and by at the citadel. I
must fetch his necessaries ashore. Farewell.
fullText: I warrant thee. Meet me by and by at the citadel. I must fetch his necessaries ashore. Farewell.

lines: That Cassio loves her, I do well believe 't.
That she loves him, 'tis apt and of great credit.
The Moor, howbeit that I endure him not,
Is of a constant, loving, noble nature,
And I dare think he'll prove to Desdemona
A most dear husband. Now, I do love her too,
Not out of absolute lust (though peradventure
I stand accountant for as great a sin)
But partly led to diet my revenge
For that I do suspect the lusty Moor
Hath leaped into my seat--the thought whereof
Doth, like a poisonous mineral, gnaw my inwards,
And nothing can or shall content my soul
Till I am evened with him, wife for wife,
Or, failing so, yet that I put the Moor
At least into a jealousy so strong
That judgment cannot cure. Which thing to do,
If this poor trash of Venice, whom I trace
For his quick hunting, stand the putting on,
I'll have our Michael Cassio on the hip,
Abuse him to the Moor in the rank garb
(For I fear Cassio with my nightcap too),
Make the Moor thank me, love me, and reward me
For making him egregiously an ass
And practicing upon his peace and quiet
Even to madness. 'Tis here, but yet confused.
Knavery's plain face is never seen till used.
fullText: That Cassio loves her, I do well believe 't. That she loves him, 'tis apt and of great credit. The Moor, howbeit that I endure him not, Is of a constant, loving, noble nature, And I dare think he'll prove to Desdemona A most dear husband. Now, I do love her too, Not out of absolute lust (though peradventure I stand accountant for as great a sin) But partly led to diet my revenge For that I do suspect the lusty Moor Hath leaped into my seat--the thought whereof Doth, like a poisonous mineral, gnaw my inwards, And nothing can or shall content my soul Till I am evened with him, wife for wife, Or, failing so, yet that I put the Moor At least into a jealousy so strong That judgment cannot cure. Which thing to do, If this poor trash of Venice, whom I trace For his quick hunting, stand the putting on, I'll have our Michael Cassio on the hip, Abuse him to the Moor in the rank garb (For I fear Cassio with my nightcap too), Make the Moor thank me, love me, and reward me For making him egregiously an ass And practicing upon his peace and quiet Even to madness. 'Tis here, but yet confused. Knavery's plain face is never seen till used.

lines: Not this hour, lieutenant. 'Tis not yet ten o' th'
clock. Our general cast us thus early for the love of
his Desdemona--who let us not therefore blame;
he hath not yet made wanton the night with her, and
she is sport for Jove.
fullText: Not this hour, lieutenant. 'Tis not yet ten o' th' clock. Our general cast us thus early for the love of his Desdemona--who let us not therefore blame; he hath not yet made wanton the night with her, and she is sport for Jove.

lines: And, I'll warrant her, full of game.
fullText: And, I'll warrant her, full of game.

lines: What an eye she has! Methinks it sounds a parley
to provocation.
fullText: What an eye she has! Methinks it sounds a parley to provocation.

lines: And when she speaks, is it not an alarum to love?
fullText: And when she speaks, is it not an alarum to love?

lines: Well, happiness to their sheets! Come, lieutenant,
I have a stoup of wine; and here without are a
brace of Cyprus gallants that would fain have a
measure to the health of black Othello.
fullText: Well, happiness to their sheets! Come, lieutenant, I have a stoup of wine; and here without are a brace of Cyprus gallants that would fain have a measure to the health of black Othello.

lines: O, they are our friends! But one cup; I'll drink
for you.
fullText: O, they are our friends! But one cup; I'll drink for you.

lines: What, man! 'Tis a night of revels. The gallants
desire it.
fullText: What, man! 'Tis a night of revels. The gallants desire it.

lines: Here at the door. I pray you, call them in.
fullText: Here at the door. I pray you, call them in.

lines: If I can fasten but one cup upon him
With that which he hath drunk tonight already,
He'll be as full of quarrel and offense
As my young mistress' dog. Now my sick fool
Roderigo,
Whom love hath turned almost the wrong side out,
To Desdemona hath tonight caroused
Potations pottle-deep; and he's to watch.
Three else of Cyprus, noble swelling spirits
That hold their honors in a wary distance,
The very elements of this warlike isle,
Have I tonight flustered with flowing cups;
And they watch too. Now, 'mongst this flock of
drunkards
Am I to put our Cassio in some action
That may offend the isle. But here they come.
If consequence do but approve my dream,
My boat sails freely both with wind and stream.
fullText: If I can fasten but one cup upon him With that which he hath drunk tonight already, He'll be as full of quarrel and offense As my young mistress' dog. Now my sick fool Roderigo, Whom love hath turned almost the wrong side out, To Desdemona hath tonight caroused Potations pottle-deep; and he's to watch. Three else of Cyprus, noble swelling spirits That hold their honors in a wary distance, The very elements of this warlike isle, Have I tonight flustered with flowing cups; And they watch too. Now, 'mongst this flock of drunkards Am I to put our Cassio in some action That may offend the isle. But here they come. If consequence do but approve my dream, My boat sails freely both with wind and stream.

lines: Some wine, ho!
And let me the cannikin clink.
A soldier's a man,
O, man's life's but a span,
Why, then, let a soldier drink.
Some wine, boys!
fullText: Some wine, ho! And let me the cannikin clink. A soldier's a man, O, man's life's but a span, Why, then, let a soldier drink. Some wine, boys!

lines: I learned it in England, where indeed they are
most potent in potting. Your Dane, your German,
and your swag-bellied Hollander--drink, ho!--are
nothing to your English.
fullText: I learned it in England, where indeed they are most potent in potting. Your Dane, your German, and your swag-bellied Hollander--drink, ho!--are nothing to your English.

lines: Why, he drinks you, with facility, your Dane
dead drunk. He sweats not to overthrow your Almain.
He gives your Hollander a vomit ere the next
pottle can be filled.
fullText: Why, he drinks you, with facility, your Dane dead drunk. He sweats not to overthrow your Almain. He gives your Hollander a vomit ere the next pottle can be filled.

lines: O sweet England!
His breeches cost him but a crown;
He held them sixpence all too dear;
With that he called the tailor lown.
He was a wight of high renown,
And thou art but of low degree;
'Tis pride that pulls the country down,
Then take thy auld cloak about thee.
Some wine, ho!
fullText: O sweet England! His breeches cost him but a crown; He held them sixpence all too dear; With that he called the tailor lown. He was a wight of high renown, And thou art but of low degree; 'Tis pride that pulls the country down, Then take thy auld cloak about thee. Some wine, ho!

lines: Will you hear 't again?
fullText: Will you hear 't again?

lines: It's true, good lieutenant.
fullText: It's true, good lieutenant.

lines: And so do I too, lieutenant.
fullText: And so do I too, lieutenant.

lines: You see this fellow that is gone before?
He's a soldier fit to stand by Caesar
And give direction; and do but see his vice.
'Tis to his virtue a just equinox,
The one as long as th' other. 'Tis pity of him.
I fear the trust Othello puts him in,
On some odd time of his infirmity,
Will shake this island.
fullText: You see this fellow that is gone before? He's a soldier fit to stand by Caesar And give direction; and do but see his vice. 'Tis to his virtue a just equinox, The one as long as th' other. 'Tis pity of him. I fear the trust Othello puts him in, On some odd time of his infirmity, Will shake this island.

lines: 'Tis evermore the prologue to his sleep.
He'll watch the horologe a double set
If drink rock not his cradle.
fullText: 'Tis evermore the prologue to his sleep. He'll watch the horologe a double set If drink rock not his cradle.

lines: How now, Roderigo?
I pray you, after the Lieutenant, go.
fullText: How now, Roderigo? I pray you, after the Lieutenant, go.

lines: Not I, for this fair island.
I do love Cassio well and would do much
To cure him of this evil--
But hark! What noise?
fullText: Not I, for this fair island. I do love Cassio well and would do much To cure him of this evil-- But hark! What noise?

lines: Away, I say! Go out and cry a mutiny.
Nay, good lieutenant.--God's will, gentlemen!--
Help, ho! Lieutenant--sir--Montano--sir--
Help, masters!--Here's a goodly watch indeed!
Who's that which rings the bell? Diablo, ho!
The town will rise. God's will, lieutenant, hold!
You will be shamed forever.
fullText: Away, I say! Go out and cry a mutiny. Nay, good lieutenant.--God's will, gentlemen!-- Help, ho! Lieutenant--sir--Montano--sir-- Help, masters!--Here's a goodly watch indeed! Who's that which rings the bell? Diablo, ho! The town will rise. God's will, lieutenant, hold! You will be shamed forever.

lines: Hold, ho! Lieutenant--sir--Montano--
gentlemen--
Have you forgot all sense of place and duty?
Hold! The General speaks to you. Hold, for shame!
fullText: Hold, ho! Lieutenant--sir--Montano-- gentlemen-- Have you forgot all sense of place and duty? Hold! The General speaks to you. Hold, for shame!

lines: I do not know. Friends all but now, even now,
In quarter and in terms like bride and groom
Divesting them for bed; and then but now,
As if some planet had unwitted men,
Swords out, and tilting one at other's breast,
In opposition bloody. I cannot speak
Any beginning to this peevish odds,
And would in action glorious I had lost
Those legs that brought me to a part of it!
fullText: I do not know. Friends all but now, even now, In quarter and in terms like bride and groom Divesting them for bed; and then but now, As if some planet had unwitted men, Swords out, and tilting one at other's breast, In opposition bloody. I cannot speak Any beginning to this peevish odds, And would in action glorious I had lost Those legs that brought me to a part of it!

lines: Touch me not so near.
I had rather have this tongue cut from my mouth
Than it should do offense to Michael Cassio.
Yet I persuade myself, to speak the truth
Shall nothing wrong him. Thus it is, general:
Montano and myself being in speech,
There comes a fellow crying out for help,
And Cassio following him with determined sword
To execute upon him. Sir, this gentleman
Steps in to Cassio and entreats his pause.
Myself the crying fellow did pursue,
Lest by his clamor--as it so fell out--
The town might fall in fright. He, swift of foot,
Outran my purpose, and I returned the rather
For that I heard the clink and fall of swords
And Cassio high in oath, which till tonight
I ne'er might say before. When I came back--
For this was brief--I found them close together
At blow and thrust, even as again they were
When you yourself did part them.
More of this matter cannot I report.
But men are men; the best sometimes forget.
Though Cassio did some little wrong to him,
As men in rage strike those that wish them best,
Yet surely Cassio, I believe, received
From him that fled some strange indignity
Which patience could not pass.
fullText: Touch me not so near. I had rather have this tongue cut from my mouth Than it should do offense to Michael Cassio. Yet I persuade myself, to speak the truth Shall nothing wrong him. Thus it is, general: Montano and myself being in speech, There comes a fellow crying out for help, And Cassio following him with determined sword To execute upon him. Sir, this gentleman Steps in to Cassio and entreats his pause. Myself the crying fellow did pursue, Lest by his clamor--as it so fell out-- The town might fall in fright. He, swift of foot, Outran my purpose, and I returned the rather For that I heard the clink and fall of swords And Cassio high in oath, which till tonight I ne'er might say before. When I came back-- For this was brief--I found them close together At blow and thrust, even as again they were When you yourself did part them. More of this matter cannot I report. But men are men; the best sometimes forget. Though Cassio did some little wrong to him, As men in rage strike those that wish them best, Yet surely Cassio, I believe, received From him that fled some strange indignity Which patience could not pass.

lines: What, are you hurt, lieutenant?
fullText: What, are you hurt, lieutenant?

lines: Marry, God forbid!
fullText: Marry, God forbid!

lines: As I am an honest man, I thought you had
received some bodily wound. There is more sense
in that than in reputation. Reputation is an idle and
most false imposition, oft got without merit and lost
without deserving. You have lost no reputation at
all, unless you repute yourself such a loser. What,
man, there are ways to recover the General again!
You are but now cast in his mood--a punishment
more in policy than in malice, even so as one would
beat his offenseless dog to affright an imperious
lion. Sue to him again and he's yours.
fullText: As I am an honest man, I thought you had received some bodily wound. There is more sense in that than in reputation. Reputation is an idle and most false imposition, oft got without merit and lost without deserving. You have lost no reputation at all, unless you repute yourself such a loser. What, man, there are ways to recover the General again! You are but now cast in his mood--a punishment more in policy than in malice, even so as one would beat his offenseless dog to affright an imperious lion. Sue to him again and he's yours.

lines: What was he that you followed with your sword?
What had he done to you?
fullText: What was he that you followed with your sword? What had he done to you?

lines: Is 't possible?
fullText: Is 't possible?

lines: Why, but you are now well enough. How came
you thus recovered?
fullText: Why, but you are now well enough. How came you thus recovered?

lines: Come, you are too severe a moraler. As the time,
the place, and the condition of this country stands,
I could heartily wish this had not so befallen. But
since it is as it is, mend it for your own good.
fullText: Come, you are too severe a moraler. As the time, the place, and the condition of this country stands, I could heartily wish this had not so befallen. But since it is as it is, mend it for your own good.

lines: Come, come, good wine is a good familiar creature,
if it be well used. Exclaim no more against it.
And, good lieutenant, I think you think I love you.
fullText: Come, come, good wine is a good familiar creature, if it be well used. Exclaim no more against it. And, good lieutenant, I think you think I love you.

lines: You or any man living may be drunk at a time,
man. I'll tell you what you shall do. Our general's
wife is now the general: I may say so in this
respect, for that he hath devoted and given up
himself to the contemplation, mark, and denotement
of her parts and graces. Confess yourself
freely to her. Importune her help to put you in your
place again. She is of so free, so kind, so apt, so
blessed a disposition she holds it a vice in her
goodness not to do more than she is requested. This
broken joint between you and her husband entreat
her to splinter, and, my fortunes against any lay
worth naming, this crack of your love shall grow
stronger than it was before.
fullText: You or any man living may be drunk at a time, man. I'll tell you what you shall do. Our general's wife is now the general: I may say so in this respect, for that he hath devoted and given up himself to the contemplation, mark, and denotement of her parts and graces. Confess yourself freely to her. Importune her help to put you in your place again. She is of so free, so kind, so apt, so blessed a disposition she holds it a vice in her goodness not to do more than she is requested. This broken joint between you and her husband entreat her to splinter, and, my fortunes against any lay worth naming, this crack of your love shall grow stronger than it was before.

lines: I protest, in the sincerity of love and honest
kindness.
fullText: I protest, in the sincerity of love and honest kindness.

lines: You are in the right. Good night, lieutenant. I
must to the watch.
fullText: You are in the right. Good night, lieutenant. I must to the watch.

lines: And what's he, then, that says I play the villain,
When this advice is free I give and honest,
Probal to thinking, and indeed the course
To win the Moor again? For 'tis most easy
Th' inclining Desdemona to subdue
In any honest suit. She's framed as fruitful
As the free elements. And then for her
To win the Moor--were 't to renounce his baptism,
All seals and symbols of redeemed sin--
His soul is so enfettered to her love
That she may make, unmake, do what she list,
Even as her appetite shall play the god
With his weak function. How am I then a villain
To counsel Cassio to this parallel course
Directly to his good? Divinity of hell!
When devils will the blackest sins put on,
They do suggest at first with heavenly shows,
As I do now. For whiles this honest fool
Plies Desdemona to repair his fortune,
And she for him pleads strongly to the Moor,
I'll pour this pestilence into his ear:
That she repeals him for her body's lust;
And by how much she strives to do him good,
She shall undo her credit with the Moor.
So will I turn her virtue into pitch,
And out of her own goodness make the net
That shall enmesh them all.
fullText: And what's he, then, that says I play the villain, When this advice is free I give and honest, Probal to thinking, and indeed the course To win the Moor again? For 'tis most easy Th' inclining Desdemona to subdue In any honest suit. She's framed as fruitful As the free elements. And then for her To win the Moor--were 't to renounce his baptism, All seals and symbols of redeemed sin-- His soul is so enfettered to her love That she may make, unmake, do what she list, Even as her appetite shall play the god With his weak function. How am I then a villain To counsel Cassio to this parallel course Directly to his good? Divinity of hell! When devils will the blackest sins put on, They do suggest at first with heavenly shows, As I do now. For whiles this honest fool Plies Desdemona to repair his fortune, And she for him pleads strongly to the Moor, I'll pour this pestilence into his ear: That she repeals him for her body's lust; And by how much she strives to do him good, She shall undo her credit with the Moor. So will I turn her virtue into pitch, And out of her own goodness make the net That shall enmesh them all.

lines: How poor are they that have not patience!
What wound did ever heal but by degrees?
Thou know'st we work by wit and not by witchcraft,
And wit depends on dilatory time.
Does 't not go well? Cassio hath beaten thee,
And thou, by that small hurt, hast cashiered Cassio.
Though other things grow fair against the sun,
Yet fruits that blossom first will first be ripe.
Content thyself awhile. By th' Mass, 'tis morning!
Pleasure and action make the hours seem short.
Retire thee; go where thou art billeted.
Away, I say! Thou shalt know more hereafter.
Nay, get thee gone.
Two things are to be done.
My wife must move for Cassio to her mistress.
I'll set her on.
Myself the while to draw the Moor apart
And bring him jump when he may Cassio find
Soliciting his wife. Ay, that's the way.
Dull not device by coldness and delay.
fullText: How poor are they that have not patience! What wound did ever heal but by degrees? Thou know'st we work by wit and not by witchcraft, And wit depends on dilatory time. Does 't not go well? Cassio hath beaten thee, And thou, by that small hurt, hast cashiered Cassio. Though other things grow fair against the sun, Yet fruits that blossom first will first be ripe. Content thyself awhile. By th' Mass, 'tis morning! Pleasure and action make the hours seem short. Retire thee; go where thou art billeted. Away, I say! Thou shalt know more hereafter. Nay, get thee gone. Two things are to be done. My wife must move for Cassio to her mistress. I'll set her on. Myself the while to draw the Moor apart And bring him jump when he may Cassio find Soliciting his wife. Ay, that's the way. Dull not device by coldness and delay.

lines: You have not been abed, then?
fullText: You have not been abed, then?

lines: I'll send her to you presently,
And I'll devise a mean to draw the Moor
Out of the way, that your converse and business
May be more free.
fullText: I'll send her to you presently, And I'll devise a mean to draw the Moor Out of the way, that your converse and business May be more free.

lines: Well, my good lord, I'll do 't.
fullText: Well, my good lord, I'll do 't.

lines: Ha, I like not that.
fullText: Ha, I like not that.

lines: Nothing, my lord; or if--I know not what.
fullText: Nothing, my lord; or if--I know not what.

lines: Cassio, my lord? No, sure, I cannot think it
That he would steal away so guiltylike,
Seeing your coming.
fullText: Cassio, my lord? No, sure, I cannot think it That he would steal away so guiltylike, Seeing your coming.

lines: My noble lord--
fullText: My noble lord--

lines: Did Michael Cassio,
When you wooed my lady, know of your love?
fullText: Did Michael Cassio, When you wooed my lady, know of your love?

lines: But for a satisfaction of my thought,
No further harm.
fullText: But for a satisfaction of my thought, No further harm.

lines: I did not think he had been acquainted with her.
fullText: I did not think he had been acquainted with her.

lines: Indeed?
fullText: Indeed?

lines: Honest, my lord?
fullText: Honest, my lord?

lines: My lord, for aught I know.
fullText: My lord, for aught I know.

lines: Think, my lord?
fullText: Think, my lord?

lines: My lord, you know I love you.
fullText: My lord, you know I love you.

lines: For Michael Cassio,
I dare be sworn I think that he is honest.
fullText: For Michael Cassio, I dare be sworn I think that he is honest.

lines: Men should be what they seem;
Or those that be not, would they might seem none!
fullText: Men should be what they seem; Or those that be not, would they might seem none!

lines: Why then, I think Cassio's an honest man.
fullText: Why then, I think Cassio's an honest man.

lines: Good my lord, pardon me.
Though I am bound to every act of duty,
I am not bound to that all slaves are free to.
Utter my thoughts? Why, say they are vile and
false--
As where's that palace whereinto foul things
Sometimes intrude not? Who has that breast so
pure
But some uncleanly apprehensions
Keep leets and law days and in sessions sit
With meditations lawful?
fullText: Good my lord, pardon me. Though I am bound to every act of duty, I am not bound to that all slaves are free to. Utter my thoughts? Why, say they are vile and false-- As where's that palace whereinto foul things Sometimes intrude not? Who has that breast so pure But some uncleanly apprehensions Keep leets and law days and in sessions sit With meditations lawful?

lines: I do beseech you,
Though I perchance am vicious in my guess--
As, I confess, it is my nature's plague
To spy into abuses, and oft my jealousy
Shapes faults that are not--that your wisdom
From one that so imperfectly conceits
Would take no notice, nor build yourself a trouble
Out of his scattering and unsure observance.
It were not for your quiet nor your good,
Nor for my manhood, honesty, and wisdom,
To let you know my thoughts.
fullText: I do beseech you, Though I perchance am vicious in my guess-- As, I confess, it is my nature's plague To spy into abuses, and oft my jealousy Shapes faults that are not--that your wisdom From one that so imperfectly conceits Would take no notice, nor build yourself a trouble Out of his scattering and unsure observance. It were not for your quiet nor your good, Nor for my manhood, honesty, and wisdom, To let you know my thoughts.

lines: Good name in man and woman, dear my lord,
Is the immediate jewel of their souls.
Who steals my purse steals trash. 'Tis something,
nothing;
'Twas mine, 'tis his, and has been slave to
thousands.
But he that filches from me my good name
Robs me of that which not enriches him
And makes me poor indeed.
fullText: Good name in man and woman, dear my lord, Is the immediate jewel of their souls. Who steals my purse steals trash. 'Tis something, nothing; 'Twas mine, 'tis his, and has been slave to thousands. But he that filches from me my good name Robs me of that which not enriches him And makes me poor indeed.

lines: You cannot, if my heart were in your hand,
Nor shall not, whilst 'tis in my custody.
fullText: You cannot, if my heart were in your hand, Nor shall not, whilst 'tis in my custody.

lines: O, beware, my lord, of jealousy!
It is the green-eyed monster which doth mock
The meat it feeds on. That cuckold lives in bliss
Who, certain of his fate, loves not his wronger;
But O, what damned minutes tells he o'er
Who dotes, yet doubts; suspects, yet strongly loves!
fullText: O, beware, my lord, of jealousy! It is the green-eyed monster which doth mock The meat it feeds on. That cuckold lives in bliss Who, certain of his fate, loves not his wronger; But O, what damned minutes tells he o'er Who dotes, yet doubts; suspects, yet strongly loves!

lines: Poor and content is rich, and rich enough;
But riches fineless is as poor as winter
To him that ever fears he shall be poor.
Good God, the souls of all my tribe defend
From jealousy!
fullText: Poor and content is rich, and rich enough; But riches fineless is as poor as winter To him that ever fears he shall be poor. Good God, the souls of all my tribe defend From jealousy!

lines: I am glad of this, for now I shall have reason
To show the love and duty that I bear you
With franker spirit. Therefore, as I am bound,
Receive it from me. I speak not yet of proof.
Look to your wife; observe her well with Cassio;
Wear your eyes thus, not jealous nor secure.
I would not have your free and noble nature,
Out of self-bounty, be abused. Look to 't.
I know our country disposition well.
In Venice they do let God see the pranks
They dare not show their husbands. Their best
conscience
Is not to leave 't undone, but keep 't unknown.
fullText: I am glad of this, for now I shall have reason To show the love and duty that I bear you With franker spirit. Therefore, as I am bound, Receive it from me. I speak not yet of proof. Look to your wife; observe her well with Cassio; Wear your eyes thus, not jealous nor secure. I would not have your free and noble nature, Out of self-bounty, be abused. Look to 't. I know our country disposition well. In Venice they do let God see the pranks They dare not show their husbands. Their best conscience Is not to leave 't undone, but keep 't unknown.

lines: She did deceive her father, marrying you,
And when she seemed to shake and fear your looks,
She loved them most.
fullText: She did deceive her father, marrying you, And when she seemed to shake and fear your looks, She loved them most.

lines: Why, go to, then!
She that, so young, could give out such a seeming,
To seel her father's eyes up close as oak,
He thought 'twas witchcraft! But I am much to
blame.
I humbly do beseech you of your pardon
For too much loving you.
fullText: Why, go to, then! She that, so young, could give out such a seeming, To seel her father's eyes up close as oak, He thought 'twas witchcraft! But I am much to blame. I humbly do beseech you of your pardon For too much loving you.

lines: I see this hath a little dashed your spirits.
fullText: I see this hath a little dashed your spirits.

lines: I' faith, I fear it has.
I hope you will consider what is spoke
Comes from my love. But I do see you're moved.
I am to pray you not to strain my speech
To grosser issues nor to larger reach
Than to suspicion.
fullText: I' faith, I fear it has. I hope you will consider what is spoke Comes from my love. But I do see you're moved. I am to pray you not to strain my speech To grosser issues nor to larger reach Than to suspicion.

lines: Should you do so, my lord,
My speech should fall into such vile success
As my thoughts aim not at. Cassio's my worthy
friend.
My lord, I see you're moved.
fullText: Should you do so, my lord, My speech should fall into such vile success As my thoughts aim not at. Cassio's my worthy friend. My lord, I see you're moved.

lines: Long live she so! And long live you to think so!
fullText: Long live she so! And long live you to think so!

lines: Ay, there's the point. As, to be bold with you,
Not to affect many proposed matches
Of her own clime, complexion, and degree,
Whereto we see in all things nature tends--
Foh! One may smell in such a will most rank,
Foul disproportion, thoughts unnatural--
But pardon me--I do not in position
Distinctly speak of her, though I may fear
Her will, recoiling to her better judgment,
May fall to match you with her country forms
And happily repent.
fullText: Ay, there's the point. As, to be bold with you, Not to affect many proposed matches Of her own clime, complexion, and degree, Whereto we see in all things nature tends-- Foh! One may smell in such a will most rank, Foul disproportion, thoughts unnatural-- But pardon me--I do not in position Distinctly speak of her, though I may fear Her will, recoiling to her better judgment, May fall to match you with her country forms And happily repent.

lines: My lord, I take my leave.
fullText: My lord, I take my leave.

lines: My lord, I would I might entreat your Honor
To scan this thing no farther. Leave it to time.
Although 'tis fit that Cassio have his place--
For sure he fills it up with great ability--
Yet, if you please to hold him off awhile,
You shall by that perceive him and his means.
Note if your lady strain his entertainment
With any strong or vehement importunity.
Much will be seen in that. In the meantime,
Let me be thought too busy in my fears--
As worthy cause I have to fear I am--
And hold her free, I do beseech your Honor.
fullText: My lord, I would I might entreat your Honor To scan this thing no farther. Leave it to time. Although 'tis fit that Cassio have his place-- For sure he fills it up with great ability-- Yet, if you please to hold him off awhile, You shall by that perceive him and his means. Note if your lady strain his entertainment With any strong or vehement importunity. Much will be seen in that. In the meantime, Let me be thought too busy in my fears-- As worthy cause I have to fear I am-- And hold her free, I do beseech your Honor.

lines: I once more take my leave.
fullText: I once more take my leave.

lines: How now? What do you here alone?
fullText: How now? What do you here alone?

lines: You have a thing for me? It is a common thing--
fullText: You have a thing for me? It is a common thing--

lines: To have a foolish wife.
fullText: To have a foolish wife.

lines: What handkerchief?
fullText: What handkerchief?

lines: Hast stol'n it from her?
fullText: Hast stol'n it from her?

lines: A good wench! Give it me.
fullText: A good wench! Give it me.

lines: Why, what is that to you?
fullText: Why, what is that to you?

lines: Be not acknown on 't.
I have use for it. Go, leave me.
I will in Cassio's lodging lose this napkin
And let him find it. Trifles light as air
Are to the jealous confirmations strong
As proofs of holy writ. This may do something.
The Moor already changes with my poison;
Dangerous conceits are in their natures poisons,
Which at the first are scarce found to distaste,
But with a little act upon the blood
Burn like the mines of sulfur.
fullText: Be not acknown on 't. I have use for it. Go, leave me. I will in Cassio's lodging lose this napkin And let him find it. Trifles light as air Are to the jealous confirmations strong As proofs of holy writ. This may do something. The Moor already changes with my poison; Dangerous conceits are in their natures poisons, Which at the first are scarce found to distaste, But with a little act upon the blood Burn like the mines of sulfur.

lines: Why, how now, general? No more of that!
fullText: Why, how now, general? No more of that!

lines: How now, my lord?
fullText: How now, my lord?

lines: I am sorry to hear this.
fullText: I am sorry to hear this.

lines: Is 't possible, my lord?
fullText: Is 't possible, my lord?

lines: Is 't come to this?
fullText: Is 't come to this?

lines: My noble lord--
fullText: My noble lord--

lines: O grace! O heaven forgive me!
Are you a man? Have you a soul or sense?
God b' wi' you. Take mine office.--O wretched fool,
That liv'st to make thine honesty a vice!--
O monstrous world! Take note, take note, O world:
To be direct and honest is not safe.--
I thank you for this profit, and from hence
I'll love no friend, sith love breeds such offense.
fullText: O grace! O heaven forgive me! Are you a man? Have you a soul or sense? God b' wi' you. Take mine office.--O wretched fool, That liv'st to make thine honesty a vice!-- O monstrous world! Take note, take note, O world: To be direct and honest is not safe.-- I thank you for this profit, and from hence I'll love no friend, sith love breeds such offense.

lines: I should be wise; for honesty's a fool
And loses that it works for.
fullText: I should be wise; for honesty's a fool And loses that it works for.

lines: I see you are eaten up with passion.
I do repent me that I put it to you.
You would be satisfied?
fullText: I see you are eaten up with passion. I do repent me that I put it to you. You would be satisfied?

lines: And may; but how? How satisfied, my lord?
Would you, the supervisor, grossly gape on,
Behold her topped?
fullText: And may; but how? How satisfied, my lord? Would you, the supervisor, grossly gape on, Behold her topped?

lines: It were a tedious difficulty, I think,
To bring them to that prospect. Damn them then
If ever mortal eyes do see them bolster
More than their own! What then? How then?
What shall I say? Where's satisfaction?
It is impossible you should see this,
Were they as prime as goats, as hot as monkeys,
As salt as wolves in pride, and fools as gross
As ignorance made drunk. But yet I say,
If imputation and strong circumstances
Which lead directly to the door of truth
Will give you satisfaction, you might have 't.
fullText: It were a tedious difficulty, I think, To bring them to that prospect. Damn them then If ever mortal eyes do see them bolster More than their own! What then? How then? What shall I say? Where's satisfaction? It is impossible you should see this, Were they as prime as goats, as hot as monkeys, As salt as wolves in pride, and fools as gross As ignorance made drunk. But yet I say, If imputation and strong circumstances Which lead directly to the door of truth Will give you satisfaction, you might have 't.

lines: I do not like the office,
But sith I am entered in this cause so far,
Pricked to 't by foolish honesty and love,
I will go on. I lay with Cassio lately,
And being troubled with a raging tooth
I could not sleep. There are a kind of men
So loose of soul that in their sleeps will mutter
Their affairs. One of this kind is Cassio.
In sleep I heard him say "Sweet Desdemona,
Let us be wary, let us hide our loves."
And then, sir, would he gripe and wring my hand,
Cry "O sweet creature!" then kiss me hard,
As if he plucked up kisses by the roots
That grew upon my lips; then laid his leg
O'er my thigh, and sighed, and kissed, and then
Cried "Cursed fate that gave thee to the Moor!"
fullText: I do not like the office, But sith I am entered in this cause so far, Pricked to 't by foolish honesty and love, I will go on. I lay with Cassio lately, And being troubled with a raging tooth I could not sleep. There are a kind of men So loose of soul that in their sleeps will mutter Their affairs. One of this kind is Cassio. In sleep I heard him say "Sweet Desdemona, Let us be wary, let us hide our loves." And then, sir, would he gripe and wring my hand, Cry "O sweet creature!" then kiss me hard, As if he plucked up kisses by the roots That grew upon my lips; then laid his leg O'er my thigh, and sighed, and kissed, and then Cried "Cursed fate that gave thee to the Moor!"

lines: Nay, this was but his
dream.
fullText: Nay, this was but his dream.

lines: And this may help to thicken other proofs
That do demonstrate thinly.
fullText: And this may help to thicken other proofs That do demonstrate thinly.

lines: Nay, but be wise. Yet we see nothing done.
She may be honest yet. Tell me but this:
Have you not sometimes seen a handkerchief
Spotted with strawberries in your wife's hand?
fullText: Nay, but be wise. Yet we see nothing done. She may be honest yet. Tell me but this: Have you not sometimes seen a handkerchief Spotted with strawberries in your wife's hand?

lines: I know not that; but such a handkerchief--
I am sure it was your wife's--did I today
See Cassio wipe his beard with.
fullText: I know not that; but such a handkerchief-- I am sure it was your wife's--did I today See Cassio wipe his beard with.

lines: If it be that, or any that was hers,
It speaks against her with the other proofs.
fullText: If it be that, or any that was hers, It speaks against her with the other proofs.

lines: Yet be content.
fullText: Yet be content.

lines: Patience, I say. Your mind perhaps may change.
fullText: Patience, I say. Your mind perhaps may change.

lines: Do not rise yet.
Witness, you ever-burning lights above,
You elements that clip us round about,
Witness that here Iago doth give up
The execution of his wit, hands, heart
To wronged Othello's service! Let him command,
And to obey shall be in me remorse,
What bloody business ever.
fullText: Do not rise yet. Witness, you ever-burning lights above, You elements that clip us round about, Witness that here Iago doth give up The execution of his wit, hands, heart To wronged Othello's service! Let him command, And to obey shall be in me remorse, What bloody business ever.

lines: My friend is dead.
'Tis done at your request. But let her live.
fullText: My friend is dead. 'Tis done at your request. But let her live.

lines: I am your own forever.
fullText: I am your own forever.

lines: There is no other way; 'tis she must do 't,
And, lo, the happiness! Go and importune her.
fullText: There is no other way; 'tis she must do 't, And, lo, the happiness! Go and importune her.

lines: Is my lord angry?
fullText: Is my lord angry?

lines: Can he be angry? I have seen the cannon
When it hath blown his ranks into the air
And, like the devil, from his very arm
Puffed his own brother--and is he angry?
Something of moment then. I will go meet him.
There's matter in 't indeed if he be angry.
fullText: Can he be angry? I have seen the cannon When it hath blown his ranks into the air And, like the devil, from his very arm Puffed his own brother--and is he angry? Something of moment then. I will go meet him. There's matter in 't indeed if he be angry.

lines: Will you think so?
fullText: Will you think so?

lines: What,
To kiss in private?
fullText: What, To kiss in private?

lines: Or to be naked with her friend in bed
An hour or more, not meaning any harm?
fullText: Or to be naked with her friend in bed An hour or more, not meaning any harm?

lines: If they do nothing, 'tis a venial slip.
But if I give my wife a handkerchief--
fullText: If they do nothing, 'tis a venial slip. But if I give my wife a handkerchief--

lines: Why then, 'tis hers, my lord, and being hers,
She may, I think, bestow 't on any man.
fullText: Why then, 'tis hers, my lord, and being hers, She may, I think, bestow 't on any man.

lines: Her honor is an essence that's not seen;
They have it very oft that have it not.
But for the handkerchief--
fullText: Her honor is an essence that's not seen; They have it very oft that have it not. But for the handkerchief--

lines: Ay, what of that?
fullText: Ay, what of that?

lines: What
If I had said I had seen him do you wrong?
Or heard him say (as knaves be such abroad,
Who having, by their own importunate suit
Or voluntary dotage of some mistress,
Convinced or supplied them, cannot choose
But they must blab)--
fullText: What If I had said I had seen him do you wrong? Or heard him say (as knaves be such abroad, Who having, by their own importunate suit Or voluntary dotage of some mistress, Convinced or supplied them, cannot choose But they must blab)--

lines: He hath, my lord, but be you well assured,
No more than he'll unswear.
fullText: He hath, my lord, but be you well assured, No more than he'll unswear.

lines: Faith, that he did--I know not what he did.
fullText: Faith, that he did--I know not what he did.

lines: Lie--
fullText: Lie--

lines: With her--on her--what you will.
fullText: With her--on her--what you will.

lines: Work on,
My medicine, work! Thus credulous fools are
caught,
And many worthy and chaste dames even thus,
All guiltless, meet reproach.--What ho! My lord!
My lord, I say. Othello!
fullText: Work on, My medicine, work! Thus credulous fools are caught, And many worthy and chaste dames even thus, All guiltless, meet reproach.--What ho! My lord! My lord, I say. Othello!

lines: My lord is fall'n into an epilepsy.
This is his second fit. He had one yesterday.
fullText: My lord is fall'n into an epilepsy. This is his second fit. He had one yesterday.

lines: No, forbear.
The lethargy must have his quiet course.
If not, he foams at mouth, and by and by
Breaks out to savage madness. Look, he stirs.
Do you withdraw yourself a little while.
He will recover straight. When he is gone,
I would on great occasion speak with you.
How is it, general? Have you not hurt your head?
fullText: No, forbear. The lethargy must have his quiet course. If not, he foams at mouth, and by and by Breaks out to savage madness. Look, he stirs. Do you withdraw yourself a little while. He will recover straight. When he is gone, I would on great occasion speak with you. How is it, general? Have you not hurt your head?

lines: I mock you not, by heaven!
Would you would bear your fortune like a man!
fullText: I mock you not, by heaven! Would you would bear your fortune like a man!

lines: There's many a beast, then, in a populous city,
And many a civil monster.
fullText: There's many a beast, then, in a populous city, And many a civil monster.

lines: Good sir, be a man!
Think every bearded fellow that's but yoked
May draw with you. There's millions now alive
That nightly lie in those unproper beds
Which they dare swear peculiar. Your case is better.
O, 'tis the spite of hell, the fiend's arch-mock,
To lip a wanton in a secure couch
And to suppose her chaste! No, let me know,
And knowing what I am, I know what she shall be.
fullText: Good sir, be a man! Think every bearded fellow that's but yoked May draw with you. There's millions now alive That nightly lie in those unproper beds Which they dare swear peculiar. Your case is better. O, 'tis the spite of hell, the fiend's arch-mock, To lip a wanton in a secure couch And to suppose her chaste! No, let me know, And knowing what I am, I know what she shall be.

lines: Stand you awhile apart.
Confine yourself but in a patient list.
Whilst you were here, o'erwhelmed with your grief--
A passion most unsuiting such a man--
Cassio came hither. I shifted him away
And laid good 'scuses upon your ecstasy,
Bade him anon return and here speak with me,
The which he promised. Do but encave yourself,
And mark the fleers, the gibes, and notable scorns
That dwell in every region of his face.
For I will make him tell the tale anew--
Where, how, how oft, how long ago, and when
He hath and is again to cope your wife.
I say but mark his gesture. Marry, patience,
Or I shall say you're all in all in spleen,
And nothing of a man.
fullText: Stand you awhile apart. Confine yourself but in a patient list. Whilst you were here, o'erwhelmed with your grief-- A passion most unsuiting such a man-- Cassio came hither. I shifted him away And laid good 'scuses upon your ecstasy, Bade him anon return and here speak with me, The which he promised. Do but encave yourself, And mark the fleers, the gibes, and notable scorns That dwell in every region of his face. For I will make him tell the tale anew-- Where, how, how oft, how long ago, and when He hath and is again to cope your wife. I say but mark his gesture. Marry, patience, Or I shall say you're all in all in spleen, And nothing of a man.

lines: That's not amiss.
But yet keep time in all. Will you withdraw?
Now will I question Cassio of Bianca,
A huswife that by selling her desires
Buys herself bread and clothes. It is a creature
That dotes on Cassio--as 'tis the strumpet's plague
To beguile many and be beguiled by one.
He, when he hears of her, cannot restrain
From the excess of laughter. Here he comes.
fullText: That's not amiss. But yet keep time in all. Will you withdraw? Now will I question Cassio of Bianca, A huswife that by selling her desires Buys herself bread and clothes. It is a creature That dotes on Cassio--as 'tis the strumpet's plague To beguile many and be beguiled by one. He, when he hears of her, cannot restrain From the excess of laughter. Here he comes.

lines: Ply Desdemona well, and you are sure on 't.
Now, if this suit lay in Bianca's power,
How quickly should you speed!
fullText: Ply Desdemona well, and you are sure on 't. Now, if this suit lay in Bianca's power, How quickly should you speed!

lines: I never knew woman love man so.
fullText: I never knew woman love man so.

lines: Do you hear, Cassio?
fullText: Do you hear, Cassio?

lines: She gives it out that you shall marry her.
Do you intend it?
fullText: She gives it out that you shall marry her. Do you intend it?

lines: Faith, the cry goes that you marry her.
fullText: Faith, the cry goes that you marry her.

lines: I am a very villain else.
fullText: I am a very villain else.

lines: Before me, look where she comes.
fullText: Before me, look where she comes.

lines: After her, after her!
fullText: After her, after her!

lines: Will you sup there?
fullText: Will you sup there?

lines: Well, I may chance to see you, for I would very
fain speak with you.
fullText: Well, I may chance to see you, for I would very fain speak with you.

lines: Go to; say no more.
fullText: Go to; say no more.

lines: Did you perceive how he laughed at his vice?
fullText: Did you perceive how he laughed at his vice?

lines: And did you see the handkerchief?
fullText: And did you see the handkerchief?

lines: Yours, by this hand! And to see how he prizes
the foolish woman your wife! She gave it him, and
he hath giv'n it his whore.
fullText: Yours, by this hand! And to see how he prizes the foolish woman your wife! She gave it him, and he hath giv'n it his whore.

lines: Nay, you must forget that.
fullText: Nay, you must forget that.

lines: Nay, that's not your way.
fullText: Nay, that's not your way.

lines: She's the worse for all this.
fullText: She's the worse for all this.

lines: Ay, too gentle.
fullText: Ay, too gentle.

lines: If you are so fond over her iniquity, give her
patent to offend, for if it touch not you, it comes
near nobody.
fullText: If you are so fond over her iniquity, give her patent to offend, for if it touch not you, it comes near nobody.

lines: O, 'tis foul in her.
fullText: O, 'tis foul in her.

lines: That's fouler.
fullText: That's fouler.

lines: Do it not with poison. Strangle her in her bed,
even the bed she hath contaminated.
fullText: Do it not with poison. Strangle her in her bed, even the bed she hath contaminated.

lines: And for Cassio, let me be his undertaker. You
shall hear more by midnight.
fullText: And for Cassio, let me be his undertaker. You shall hear more by midnight.

lines: I warrant something from Venice.
fullText: I warrant something from Venice.

lines: I am very glad to see you, signior.
Welcome to Cyprus.
fullText: I am very glad to see you, signior. Welcome to Cyprus.

lines: Lives, sir.
fullText: Lives, sir.

lines: He is much
changed.
fullText: He is much changed.

lines: He's that he is. I may not breathe my censure
What he might be. If what he might he is not,
I would to heaven he were.
fullText: He's that he is. I may not breathe my censure What he might be. If what he might he is not, I would to heaven he were.

lines: 'Faith, that was not so well. Yet would I knew
That stroke would prove the worst.
fullText: 'Faith, that was not so well. Yet would I knew That stroke would prove the worst.

lines: Alas, alas!
It is not honesty in me to speak
What I have seen and known. You shall observe
him,
And his own courses will denote him so
That I may save my speech. Do but go after
And mark how he continues.
fullText: Alas, alas! It is not honesty in me to speak What I have seen and known. You shall observe him, And his own courses will denote him so That I may save my speech. Do but go after And mark how he continues.

lines: What is your pleasure, madam? How is 't with you?
fullText: What is your pleasure, madam? How is 't with you?

lines: What is the matter, lady?
fullText: What is the matter, lady?

lines: What name, fair
lady?
fullText: What name, fair lady?

lines: Why did he so?
fullText: Why did he so?

lines: Do not weep, do not weep! Alas the day!
fullText: Do not weep, do not weep! Alas the day!

lines: Beshrew him for 't! How comes this trick upon him?
fullText: Beshrew him for 't! How comes this trick upon him?

lines: Fie, there is no such man. It is impossible.
fullText: Fie, there is no such man. It is impossible.

lines: Speak within door.
fullText: Speak within door.

lines: You are a fool. Go to!
fullText: You are a fool. Go to!

lines: I pray you be content. 'Tis but his humor.
The business of the state does him offense,
And he does chide with you.
fullText: I pray you be content. 'Tis but his humor. The business of the state does him offense, And he does chide with you.

lines: It is but so, I warrant.
Hark how these instruments summon to supper.
The messengers of Venice stays the meat.
Go in and weep not. All things shall be well.
fullText: It is but so, I warrant. Hark how these instruments summon to supper. The messengers of Venice stays the meat. Go in and weep not. All things shall be well.

lines: What in the contrary?
fullText: What in the contrary?

lines: Will you hear me, Roderigo?
fullText: Will you hear me, Roderigo?

lines: You charge me most unjustly.
fullText: You charge me most unjustly.

lines: Well, go to! Very well.
fullText: Well, go to! Very well.

lines: Very well.
fullText: Very well.

lines: You have said now.
fullText: You have said now.

lines: Why, now I see there's mettle in thee, and even
from this instant do build on thee a better opinion
than ever before. Give me thy hand, Roderigo.
Thou hast taken against me a most just exception,
but yet I protest I have dealt most directly in thy
affair.
fullText: Why, now I see there's mettle in thee, and even from this instant do build on thee a better opinion than ever before. Give me thy hand, Roderigo. Thou hast taken against me a most just exception, but yet I protest I have dealt most directly in thy affair.

lines: I grant indeed it hath not appeared, and your
suspicion is not without wit and judgment. But,
Roderigo, if thou hast that in thee indeed which I
have greater reason to believe now than ever--I
mean purpose, courage, and valor--this night show
it. If thou the next night following enjoy not Desdemona,
take me from this world with treachery and
devise engines for my life.
fullText: I grant indeed it hath not appeared, and your suspicion is not without wit and judgment. But, Roderigo, if thou hast that in thee indeed which I have greater reason to believe now than ever--I mean purpose, courage, and valor--this night show it. If thou the next night following enjoy not Desdemona, take me from this world with treachery and devise engines for my life.

lines: Sir, there is especial commission come from
Venice to depute Cassio in Othello's place.
fullText: Sir, there is especial commission come from Venice to depute Cassio in Othello's place.

lines: O, no. He goes into Mauritania and takes away
with him the fair Desdemona, unless his abode be
lingered here by some accident--wherein none
can be so determinate as the removing of Cassio.
fullText: O, no. He goes into Mauritania and takes away with him the fair Desdemona, unless his abode be lingered here by some accident--wherein none can be so determinate as the removing of Cassio.

lines: Why, by making him uncapable of Othello's
place: knocking out his brains.
fullText: Why, by making him uncapable of Othello's place: knocking out his brains.

lines: Ay, if you dare do yourself a profit and a right. He
sups tonight with a harlotry, and thither will I go to
him. He knows not yet of his honorable fortune. If
you will watch his going thence (which I will
fashion to fall out between twelve and one), you may
take him at your pleasure. I will be near to second
your attempt, and he shall fall between us. Come,
stand not amazed at it, but go along with me. I will
show you such a necessity in his death that you shall
think yourself bound to put it on him. It is now high
supper time, and the night grows to waste. About it!
fullText: Ay, if you dare do yourself a profit and a right. He sups tonight with a harlotry, and thither will I go to him. He knows not yet of his honorable fortune. If you will watch his going thence (which I will fashion to fall out between twelve and one), you may take him at your pleasure. I will be near to second your attempt, and he shall fall between us. Come, stand not amazed at it, but go along with me. I will show you such a necessity in his death that you shall think yourself bound to put it on him. It is now high supper time, and the night grows to waste. About it!

lines: And you shall be satisfied.
fullText: And you shall be satisfied.

lines: Here, stand behind this bulk. Straight will he
come.
Wear thy good rapier bare, and put it home.
Quick, quick! Fear nothing. I'll be at thy elbow.
It makes us or it mars us--think on that,
And fix most firm thy resolution.
fullText: Here, stand behind this bulk. Straight will he come. Wear thy good rapier bare, and put it home. Quick, quick! Fear nothing. I'll be at thy elbow. It makes us or it mars us--think on that, And fix most firm thy resolution.

lines: Here, at thy hand. Be bold and take thy stand.
fullText: Here, at thy hand. Be bold and take thy stand.

lines: I have rubbed this young quat almost to the sense,
And he grows angry. Now, whether he kill Cassio,
Or Cassio him, or each do kill the other,
Every way makes my gain. Live Roderigo,
He calls me to a restitution large
Of gold and jewels that I bobbed from him
As gifts to Desdemona.
It must not be. If Cassio do remain,
He hath a daily beauty in his life
That makes me ugly. And besides, the Moor
May unfold me to him. There stand I in much peril.
No, he must die. Be 't so. I hear him coming.
fullText: I have rubbed this young quat almost to the sense, And he grows angry. Now, whether he kill Cassio, Or Cassio him, or each do kill the other, Every way makes my gain. Live Roderigo, He calls me to a restitution large Of gold and jewels that I bobbed from him As gifts to Desdemona. It must not be. If Cassio do remain, He hath a daily beauty in his life That makes me ugly. And besides, the Moor May unfold me to him. There stand I in much peril. No, he must die. Be 't so. I hear him coming.

lines: Who's there? Whose noise is this that cries on
murder?
fullText: Who's there? Whose noise is this that cries on murder?

lines: Did not you hear a cry?
fullText: Did not you hear a cry?

lines: What's the matter?
fullText: What's the matter?

lines: What are you here that cry so grievously?
fullText: What are you here that cry so grievously?

lines: O me, lieutenant! What villains have done this?
fullText: O me, lieutenant! What villains have done this?

lines: O treacherous villains!
Come in, and give some help.
fullText: O treacherous villains! Come in, and give some help.

lines: O murd'rous slave! O villain!
fullText: O murd'rous slave! O villain!

lines: Kill men i' th' dark?--Where be these bloody
thieves?
How silent is this town! Ho, murder, murder!--
What may you be? Are you of good or evil?
fullText: Kill men i' th' dark?--Where be these bloody thieves? How silent is this town! Ho, murder, murder!-- What may you be? Are you of good or evil?

lines: Signior Lodovico?
fullText: Signior Lodovico?

lines: I cry you mercy. Here's Cassio hurt by villains.
fullText: I cry you mercy. Here's Cassio hurt by villains.

lines: How is 't, brother?
fullText: How is 't, brother?

lines: Marry, heaven forbid!
Light, gentlemen. I'll bind it with my shirt.
fullText: Marry, heaven forbid! Light, gentlemen. I'll bind it with my shirt.

lines: Who is 't that cried?
fullText: Who is 't that cried?

lines: O notable strumpet! Cassio, may you suspect
Who they should be that have thus mangled you?
fullText: O notable strumpet! Cassio, may you suspect Who they should be that have thus mangled you?

lines: Lend me a garter. So.--O for a chair
To bear him easily hence!
fullText: Lend me a garter. So.--O for a chair To bear him easily hence!

lines: Gentlemen all, I do suspect this trash
To be a party in this injury.--
Patience awhile, good Cassio.--Come, come;
Lend me a light. Know we this
face or no?
Alas, my friend and my dear countryman
Roderigo? No! Yes, sure. O heaven, Roderigo!
fullText: Gentlemen all, I do suspect this trash To be a party in this injury.-- Patience awhile, good Cassio.--Come, come; Lend me a light. Know we this face or no? Alas, my friend and my dear countryman Roderigo? No! Yes, sure. O heaven, Roderigo!

lines: Even he, sir. Did you know him?
fullText: Even he, sir. Did you know him?

lines: Signior Gratiano? I cry your gentle pardon.
These bloody accidents must excuse my manners
That so neglected you.
fullText: Signior Gratiano? I cry your gentle pardon. These bloody accidents must excuse my manners That so neglected you.

lines: How do you, Cassio?--O, a chair, a chair!
fullText: How do you, Cassio?--O, a chair, a chair!

lines: He, he, 'tis he! O, that's well
said; the chair.--
Some good man bear him carefully from hence.
I'll fetch the General's surgeon.-- For you, mistress,
Save you your labor.--He that lies slain here,
Cassio,
Was my dear friend. What malice was between you?
fullText: He, he, 'tis he! O, that's well said; the chair.-- Some good man bear him carefully from hence. I'll fetch the General's surgeon.-- For you, mistress, Save you your labor.--He that lies slain here, Cassio, Was my dear friend. What malice was between you?

lines: What, look you pale?--O, bear him out o' th' air.
gentlemen.--Look you pale, mistress?--
Do you perceive the gastness of her eye?--
Nay, if you stare, we shall hear more anon.--
Behold her well. I pray you, look upon her.
Do you see, gentlemen? Nay, guiltiness will speak
Though tongues were out of use.
fullText: What, look you pale?--O, bear him out o' th' air. gentlemen.--Look you pale, mistress?-- Do you perceive the gastness of her eye?-- Nay, if you stare, we shall hear more anon.-- Behold her well. I pray you, look upon her. Do you see, gentlemen? Nay, guiltiness will speak Though tongues were out of use.

lines: Cassio hath here been set on in the dark
By Roderigo and fellows that are scaped.
He's almost slain, and Roderigo dead.
fullText: Cassio hath here been set on in the dark By Roderigo and fellows that are scaped. He's almost slain, and Roderigo dead.

lines: This is the fruits of whoring. Prithee, Emilia,
Go know of Cassio where he supped tonight.
fullText: This is the fruits of whoring. Prithee, Emilia, Go know of Cassio where he supped tonight.

lines: O, did he so? I charge you go with me.
fullText: O, did he so? I charge you go with me.

lines: Kind gentlemen, let's go see poor Cassio dressed.--
Come, mistress, you must tell 's another tale.--
Emilia, run you to the citadel
And tell my lord and lady what hath happed.--
Will you go on afore? This is the night
That either makes me or fordoes me quite.
fullText: Kind gentlemen, let's go see poor Cassio dressed.-- Come, mistress, you must tell 's another tale.-- Emilia, run you to the citadel And tell my lord and lady what hath happed.-- Will you go on afore? This is the night That either makes me or fordoes me quite.

lines: I told him what I thought, and told no more
Than what he found himself was apt and true.
fullText: I told him what I thought, and told no more Than what he found himself was apt and true.

lines: I did.
fullText: I did.

lines: With Cassio, mistress. Go to! Charm your tongue.
fullText: With Cassio, mistress. Go to! Charm your tongue.

lines: What, are you mad? I charge you get you home.
fullText: What, are you mad? I charge you get you home.

lines: Zounds, hold your peace!
fullText: Zounds, hold your peace!

lines: Be wise, and get you home.
fullText: Be wise, and get you home.

lines: Villainous whore!
fullText: Villainous whore!

lines: Filth, thou liest!
fullText: Filth, thou liest!

lines: I bleed, sir, but not killed.
fullText: I bleed, sir, but not killed.

lines: Demand me nothing. What you know, you know.
From this time forth I never will speak word.
fullText: Demand me nothing. What you know, you know. From this time forth I never will speak word.
